subject: Chemistry
q: Which one of the following species responds to an external magnetic field?
question: Which one of the following species responds to an external magnetic field?
solution:
1. FeH2O63+

Fe3+ : Ar3d5

Hybridisation : sp3d2

Magnetic nature : Paramagnetic (so this complex response to external magnetic field)

2. NiCN42-

Ni3+ : Ar3d8

Hybridisation : dsp2

Magnetic nature : diamagnetic

3. CoCN63-

Co3+ : Ar3d6

Hybridisation : d2sp3

Magnetic nature : diamagnetic

4. NiCO4

Ni : Ar3d8 4s2

Hybridisation : sp3

Magnetic nature : diamagnetic

subject: Chemistry
q:
The spin-only magnetic moment value for the complex Co(CN)64- is...... BM. (nearest integer value)
[At. no. of Co=27]
question:
The spin-only magnetic moment value for the complex Co(CN)64- is...... BM. (nearest integer value)
[At. no. of Co=27]
solution:
Co(CN)64-
x+6×(-1)=-4
x=+2
Co2+:[Ar]3 d7 and CN- is a strong field ligand which can pair electron of central atom.

It has one unpaired electron (n) in 4 d-subshell. So spin only magnetic moment (μ)=
n(n+2)B·M
where n= number of unpaired electrons.
μ=3 B.M
μ=1.73 BM ≃2 BM

q:
Arrange the following Cobalt complexes in the order of increasing Crystal Field Stabilization Energy (CFSE) value.

Complexes: CoF63-A,CoH2O62+B,CoNH363+C and Co(en)33+D

Choose the correct option :
question:
Arrange the following Cobalt complexes in the order of increasing Crystal Field Stabilization Energy (CFSE) value.

Complexes: CoF63-A,CoH2O62+B,CoNH363+C and Co(en)33+D

Choose the correct option :
solution:
CFSE ∝ Strength of ligand

 en>NH3>H2O>F-

CFSE ∝ Oxidation number of the central metal.

∴ Order of CFSE

CoH2O62+<CoF63-<CoNH363+<Co(en)33+

q: Homoleptic octahedral complexes of a metal ion <math><mi>′</mi><msup><mrow><mi>M</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup><mi>′</mi></math> with three monodentate ligands <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>L</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi>L</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mi> </mi><msub><mi>L</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></math> absorb wavelengths in the region of green, blue and red respectively. The increasing order of the ligand strength is:
question: Homoleptic octahedral complexes of a metal ion <math><mi>′</mi><msup><mrow><mi>M</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup><mi>′</mi></math> with three monodentate ligands <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>L</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi>L</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mi> </mi><msub><mi>L</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></math> absorb wavelengths in the region of green, blue and red respectively. The increasing order of the ligand strength is:
solution:
Observed order of wavelengths, λabs =L3>L1>L2.

(Red light has highest wavelength of 700 nm while blue is the shortest with a wavelength of about 480 nm)

So, ∆0 (strength of ligands) order  will be L2>L1>L3.

(As ∆0 α 1λabs )

Hence, order will be L2>L1>L3.

Therefore, Strength of ligand ∝∆Absorption.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Which of the following are true about the energy of the given d-orbitals of a tetrahedral complex?
A. $d_{xy} = d_{xz} > d_{x^2-y^2}$
B. $d_{xy} = d_{yz} > d_{z^2}$
C. $d_{x^2-y^2} > d_{z^2} > d_{xz}$
D. $d_{x^2-y^2} = d_{z^2} < d_{xz}$

Choose the correct answer from the given below:
question:
Which of the following are true about the energy of the given d-orbitals of a tetrahedral complex?
A. $d_{xy} = d_{xz} > d_{x^2-y^2}$
B. $d_{xy} = d_{yz} > d_{z^2}$
C. $d_{x^2-y^2} > d_{z^2} > d_{xz}$
D. $d_{x^2-y^2} = d_{z^2} < d_{xz}$

Choose the correct answer from the given below:
solution:
In a tetrahedral complex, the five d-orbitals split into two sets due to the crystal field created by the ligands.

The ligands approach the central metal ion between the axes. The $d_{xy}$, $d_{yz}$, and $d_{xz}$ orbitals (forming the $t_2$ set) point between the axes, experiencing greater repulsion from the ligands, and thus have higher energy.

The $d_{x^2-y^2}$ and $d_{z^2}$ orbitals (forming the $e$ set) point along the axes, experiencing lesser repulsion, and thus have lower energy.

Therefore, the energy order is $d_{xy} = d_{yz} = d_{xz} > d_{x^2-y^2} = d_{z^2}$.

Evaluating the given statements:
A. $d_{xy} = d_{xz} > d_{x^2-y^2}$ is true.
B. $d_{xy} = d_{yz} > d_{z^2}$ is true.
C. $d_{x^2-y^2} > d_{z^2} > d_{xz}$ is false.
D. $d_{x^2-y^2} = d_{z^2} < d_{xz}$ is true.

Hence, statements A, B, and D are true.

Answer: A, B and D only

q: Which of the following complex is octahedral, diamagnetic and the most stable?
question: Which of the following complex is octahedral, diamagnetic and the most stable?
solution:
The coordination compound K3[Co(CN)6]contains a cobalt(III) ion, which has a d6 electronic configuration. The cyanide ligands in this complex are indeed strong field ligands, which means they will cause a large splitting of the d orbitals. So here cobalt is in +3 state having 3d6 configuration and also cyanide ligand which is a strong field ligand so, it is most stable and will have octahedral shape. The given compounds have the following nature:

(1) Na3[CoCl6]  – Paramagnetic

(2) NiNH36Cl2 – Paramagnetic

(3) K3Co(CN)6– Diamagnetic

(4) CoH2O6Cl2 – Paramagnetic

q:
Consider the following reaction and statements:

CoNH34Br2++Br-→CoNH33Br3+NH3

(i) Two isomers are produced if the reactant complex ion is a cis-isomer.

(ii) Two isomers are produced if the reactant complex ion is a trans-isomer.

(iii) Only one isomer is produced if the reactant complex ion is a trans-isomer.

(iv) Only one isomer is produced if the reactant complex ion is a cis-isomer.

The correct statements are
question:
Consider the following reaction and statements:

CoNH34Br2++Br-→CoNH33Br3+NH3

(i) Two isomers are produced if the reactant complex ion is a cis-isomer.

(ii) Two isomers are produced if the reactant complex ion is a trans-isomer.

(iii) Only one isomer is produced if the reactant complex ion is a trans-isomer.

(iv) Only one isomer is produced if the reactant complex ion is a cis-isomer.

The correct statements are
solution:
From the below image it is clear that two isomers are produced if the reactant complexion is a cis-isomer, as well as only one isomer, is produced if the reactant complex is a trans-isomer.

Two isomers are produced if the reactant complex ion is a cis-isomer because the cis isomer can further form facial and meridional isomers.

Only one isomer is produced if the reactant complex ion is a trans-isomer because it cannot form any isomeric isomers.

Trans → only meridonial

Cis → fac and mer

subject: Chemistry
q:
Which of the following complexes will show coordination isomerism?
A. $[Ag(NH_3)_2][Ag(CN)_2]$
B. $[Co(NH_3)_6][Cr(CN)_6]$
C. $[Co(NH_3)_6][Co(CN)_6]$
D. $[Fe(NH_3)_6][Co(CN)_6]$
E. $[Co(NH_3)_6][Fe(CN)_6]$
Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
Which of the following complexes will show coordination isomerism?
A. $[Ag(NH_3)_2][Ag(CN)_2]$
B. $[Co(NH_3)_6][Cr(CN)_6]$
C. $[Co(NH_3)_6][Co(CN)_6]$
D. $[Fe(NH_3)_6][Co(CN)_6]$
E. $[Co(NH_3)_6][Fe(CN)_6]$
Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
solution:
Coordination isomerism arises from the interchange of ligands between cationic and anionic entities of different metal ions present in a complex.

A. $[Ag(NH_3)_2][Ag(CN)_2]$: Both the complex cation and anion contain the same metal ion ($Ag^+$). Interchanging one ligand produces a neutral molecule $[Ag(NH_3)(CN)]$, and interchanging both ligands yields the identical compound. Thus, it does not show coordination isomerism.

B. $[Co(NH_3)_6][Cr(CN)_6]$: The complex contains different metal ions ($Co^{3+}$ and $Cr^{3+}$). Interchange of ligands gives the coordination isomer $[Cr(NH_3)_6][Co(CN)_6]$. Thus, it shows coordination isomerism.

C. $[Co(NH_3)_6][Co(CN)_6]$: Both the complex cation and anion contain the same metal ion ($Co^{3+}$). According to the strict definition requiring different metal ions, this is generally excluded from being a primary example of coordination isomerism in standard contexts.

D. $[Fe(NH_3)_6][Co(CN)_6]$: The complex contains different metal ions ($Fe^{3+}$ and $Co^{3+}$). Interchange of ligands gives the coordination isomer $[Co(NH_3)_6][Fe(CN)_6]$. Thus, it shows coordination isomerism.

E. $[Co(NH_3)_6][Fe(CN)_6]$: This is the coordination isomer of complex D. Since it contains different metal ions, it also shows coordination isomerism by interchanging ligands to form $[Fe(NH_3)_6][Co(CN)_6]$.

Since complexes D and E are coordination isomers of each other, they must both be included together. Therefore, the complexes that show coordination isomerism are B, D, and E.

Answer: B, D and E Only

q: Identify from the following species in which <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><msup><mi>sp</mi><mn>3</mn></msup></math> hybridization is shown by central atom:
question: Identify from the following species in which <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><msup><mi>sp</mi><mn>3</mn></msup></math> hybridization is shown by central atom:
solution:
CoNH36+3-d2sp3 hybridization

In CoNH363+, Co is in +3 oxidation state with the configuration 3d6. In the presence of NH3 a strong ligand, the 3d electrons pair up leaving two d-orbitals empty. Hence, the hybridization is d2sp3forming an inner orbital octahedral complex. 

BrF5-sp3d2 hybridization

PtCl4-2-dsp2 hybridization

SF6-sp3d2 hybridization

q:
$\mathrm{CrCl}_3 \cdot \mathrm{xNH}_3$ can exist as a complex. 0.1 molal aqueous solution of this complex shows a depression in freezing point of $0.558^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Assuming $100 \%$ ionisation of this complex and coordination number of Cr is 6 , the complex will be
(Given $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}}=1.86 \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ )
question:
$\mathrm{CrCl}_3 \cdot \mathrm{xNH}_3$ can exist as a complex. 0.1 molal aqueous solution of this complex shows a depression in freezing point of $0.558^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Assuming $100 \%$ ionisation of this complex and coordination number of Cr is 6 , the complex will be
(Given $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}}=1.86 \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ )
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \Delta T_f=i K_f m \\ & 0.558=i \times 1.86 \times 0.1 \\ & i=\frac{0.558}{0.186}=3\end{aligned}$
Number of ions when 100\% ionisation takes place $=3$
$\left[\mathrm{Cr}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_5 \mathrm{Cl}\right] \mathrm{Cl}_2 \xrightarrow{\text { aqueous solution }}\left[\mathrm{Cr}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_5 \mathrm{Cl}^{2+}\right]^{2+}+2 \mathrm{Cl}^{-}$
Number of ions $=3$

→2 unpaired electron

solution:
As the oxidation state of metal increases the CFSE value increases hence  Δ of [Ti(H2O)6]3+>[Ti(H2O)6]2+

  Δ increase from 3d to 4d Series. So,   

Δ ∝ CFSE (Crystal field stabilisation energy)

Δ of CrH2O62+<Δ of MoH2O62+

Because here Δ depends on Zeff & Zeff of 4d series is more than 3d series

subject: Chemistry
q: Wilkinson catalyst is
question: Wilkinson catalyst is
solution: Chlorotris(triphenylphosphine)rhodium(<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></math>) is known as Wilkinson's catalyst. It is used as a homogeneous hydrogenation catalyst. It is a square planar <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>16</mn><mo>-</mo></math>electron complex. The oxidation state of Rhodium in it is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></math>.

subject: Chemistry
q:
The correct order of the complexes $\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_5\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)\right]^{3+}$ (A),
$\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_6\right]^{3+}$ (B),
$\left[\mathrm{Co}(\mathrm{CN})_6\right]^{3-}(\mathrm{C})$ and $\left[\mathrm{CoCl}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_5\right]^{2+}(\mathrm{D})$ in terms wavelength of light absorbed is :
question:
The correct order of the complexes $\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_5\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)\right]^{3+}$ (A),
$\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_6\right]^{3+}$ (B),
$\left[\mathrm{Co}(\mathrm{CN})_6\right]^{3-}(\mathrm{C})$ and $\left[\mathrm{CoCl}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_5\right]^{2+}(\mathrm{D})$ in terms wavelength of light absorbed is :
solution:
We know $\mathrm{E}=\mathrm{h} v=\frac{\mathrm{hC}}{\lambda}$
$\mathrm{E} \propto \frac{1}{\lambda}$
Here all Co in +3 oxidation state.
So, as the ligand field strength $\uparrow, \operatorname{CFSE} \uparrow$
Order of field strength of ligand :
$\mathrm{CN} \gt \mathrm{NH}_3 \gt \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \gt \mathrm{Cl}^{-}$
CFSE order : $\mathrm{C} \gt \mathrm{B} \gt \mathrm{A} \gt \mathrm{D}$
Wavelength order: $\mathrm{D} \gt \mathrm{A} \gt \mathrm{B} \gt \mathrm{C}$

q: The volume (in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>mL</mi></math>) of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mo> </mo><msub><mi>AgNO</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></math> required for complete precipitation of chloride ions present in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>01</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math> solution of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mi>Cr</mi><msub><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow></mfenced><mn>5</mn></msub><mi>Cl</mi></mrow></mfenced><msub><mi>Cl</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> as silver chloride is_______
question: The volume (in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>mL</mi></math>) of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mo> </mo><msub><mi>AgNO</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></math> required for complete precipitation of chloride ions present in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>01</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math> solution of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mi>Cr</mi><msub><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow></mfenced><mn>5</mn></msub><mi>Cl</mi></mrow></mfenced><msub><mi>Cl</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> as silver chloride is_______
solution:
CrH2O5ClCl2⟶CrH2O5Cl+2Cl-

Number of ionisable Cl− in CrH2O5ClCl2​ is 2.

Molarity of Cl– ions =0.01× 2=0.02 M

∴ Millimoles of Cl– ions in the given solution

=20×0.02

∴ Millimoles of AgNO3 required =20×0.2

V×0.1=20×0.02

V=4 mL

subject: Chemistry
q:
Choose the correct Statements from the following:
(A) Ethane-1, 2-diamine is a chelating ligand.
(B) Metallic aluminium is produced by electrolysis of aluminium oxide in presence of cryolite.
(C) Cyanide ion is used as ligand for leaching of silver.
(D) Phosphine act as a ligand in Wilkinson catalyst.
(E) The stability constants of Ca2+ and Mg2+ are similar with EDTA complexes.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
Choose the correct Statements from the following:
(A) Ethane-1, 2-diamine is a chelating ligand.
(B) Metallic aluminium is produced by electrolysis of aluminium oxide in presence of cryolite.
(C) Cyanide ion is used as ligand for leaching of silver.
(D) Phosphine act as a ligand in Wilkinson catalyst.
(E) The stability constants of Ca2+ and Mg2+ are similar with EDTA complexes.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
solution:
Statement A

Ethane−1,2−diamine is a chelating agent as it contains two coordinating sites that are two nitrogen atoms which donate their lone pairs of electrons and can form a 5-membered ring with the metal ion.

Statement B

Based on Hall-Heroults process-

The Hall-Heroult process is widely used in the extraction of aluminium. In Hall-Heroults process, pure Al2O3 is mixed with CaF2 or Na3AlF6. This results in lowering the melting point of the mixture and increases its ability to conduct electricity.  A steel vessel with a lining of carbon and graphite rods is used.

Statement C

Cyanide ion is used as ligand for leaching of silver.

The cyanide process, which is also known as the Macarthur-forest process, is widely used in extracting gold or silver from the ores by dissolving them in a dilute solution of potassium cyanide or sodium cyanide.

Statement D 

RhPPh33Cl Wilkinson's catalyst

Statement E

Ca++ ion forms more stable complex with EDTA

Hence option C is the answer.

subject: Chemistry
q:
If Ni2+ is replaced by Pt2+ in the complex NiCl2Br22-, which of the following properties are expected to get changed?
A. Geometry
B. Geometrical isomerism
C. Optical isomerism
D. Magnetic properties
question:
If Ni2+ is replaced by Pt2+ in the complex NiCl2Br22-, which of the following properties are expected to get changed?
A. Geometry
B. Geometrical isomerism
C. Optical isomerism
D. Magnetic properties
solution:
When Ni2+ is replaced by Pt2+ in the complex NiCl2Br22-, the following properties are expected to be changed:

A. Geometry: Since Pt2+ and Ni2+ have different coordination preferences and atomic sizes, the replacement is likely to result in a change in the geometry of the complex. That is it changes to square planar.

B. Geometrical isomerism: If the replacement of Ni2+ with Pt2+ leads to a change in the ligand arrangement, it can result in the generation of cis and trans isomers.

C. Magnetic properties: PtCl2Br22- is diamagnetic.

Both the complex species are optically inactive.

question: The spin only magnetic moment of a divalent ion in aqueous solution (atomic number <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>29</mn></math>) is ___ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>BM</mi></math>.
solution:
Z=29Cu

Cu2+ form CuH2O42+ complex ion with H2O

CuH2O42+⇒Cu2+Ar3 d9,H2O→WFL

number of unpaired e-=1

μ=11+2B.M

μ=3⇒1.73 B.M.⇒round off ans. ⇒2

subject: Chemistry
q: The hybridization and magnetic behaviour of cobalt ion in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>[</mo><mi>Co</mi><mo>(</mo><msub><mi>NH</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><msub><mo>)</mo><mn>6</mn></msub><msup><mo>]</mo><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup></math> complex, respectively is
question: The hybridization and magnetic behaviour of cobalt ion in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>[</mo><mi>Co</mi><mo>(</mo><msub><mi>NH</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><msub><mo>)</mo><mn>6</mn></msub><msup><mo>]</mo><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup></math> complex, respectively is
solution:
In [Co(NH3)6]3+, Co is in +3 oxidation state with the configuration 3d6. In the presence of NH3 a strong field ligand, the 3d electrons undergo pairing and  leaving two d-orbitals empty. Hence, the hybridisation is d2sp3 which forms an inner orbital octahedral complex. The complex is diamagnetic as it has no unpaired electrons.

So, the correct option is C

subject: Chemistry
q: The IUPAC name of the complex <math><mfenced close="]" open="[" separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><msub><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></mfenced></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi></math> is
question: The IUPAC name of the complex <math><mfenced close="]" open="[" separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><msub><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></mfenced></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi></math> is
options: Bisammine (methanamine) chlorido platinum <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> chloride
solution:
NH 3  is neutral ligand, its IUPAC name is ammine

Cl  is negative ligand, its IUPAC name is chlrido

NH 2 CH 3  is neutral ligand, its IUPAC name is methanamine

Oxidation state of platinum ⇒ x+2×0+1 −1 +1 0 =1

x=2
Naming of ligand should be done in the alphabetically order.

The IUPAC name of the complex is Diamminechlorido (methanamine) platinum II chloride

subject: Chemistry
q:
(a) CoCl3.4NH3,

(b) CoCl3·5NH3,

(c) CoCl3·6NH3 and

(d) CoClNO32·5NH3

Number of complex(es) which will exist in cis-trans form is/are
question:
(a) CoCl3.4NH3,

(b) CoCl3·5NH3,

(c) CoCl3·6NH3 and

(d) CoClNO32·5NH3

Number of complex(es) which will exist in cis-trans form is/are
solution:
Cis-trans isomerism is a type of geometrical isomerism.This type of isomerism occurs when there is a change in spatial arrangements of ligands. Cis and trans are the two terms used for representing the position of identical ligands when they are placed adjacent and opposite

(i) CoCl3.4NH3⇒CoNH34Cl2Cl, can exhibit cis-trans isomerism.

(ii) CoCl3.5NH3⇒CoNH35ClCl2,can't exhibit cis-trans  isomerism.

(iii) CoCl3.6NH3⇒CoNH36Cl3,can't exhibit cis-trans  isomerism.

(IV)

 CoClNO32.5NH3⇒CoNH35ClNO32 or CoNH35NO3Cl NO3

Both can't exhibit cis-trans  isomerism.

Hence. only one complex exhibits cis-trans isomerism

subject: Chemistry
q: Which one of the following complexes is violet in colour?
question: Which one of the following complexes is violet in colour?
solution:
Sulphur of organic compounds is detected by using sodium nitroprusside. With it sulphur forms a violet colour complex, called sodium thionitroprusside.

S2-+Na2Fe(CN)5(NO)+Sodium nitroprusside→Na4Fe(CN)5(NOS)Violet colour
